ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken curry' class (FOODON_03302757); no specific Kundapuri or Mangalorean coconut chicken curry class exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Kundapuri chicken curry entry. A0CKN (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent node. Coconut and spice matrix not captured at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kundapuri chicken curry or Mangalorean chicken curry at the time of processing.

main_ingredients: whole spices (coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, cloves, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kori Kundapuri is approximately 300 g, comprising boneless chicken pieces in a generous coconut-based curry sauce, suitable as a main course for one person.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, with coconut milk – Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for chicken curry with coconut milk, which is the closest approved-source match to Kori Kundapuri. Kundapuri-specific values are not separately tabulated. Values are adapted from the coconut-based chicken curry entry. Coconut oil and red chilli content are consistent with the Kundapuri preparation style. Minor adjustments noted in the notes field.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed chicken curry dishes – analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate coconut-based curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kori Kundapuri in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous coconut-based chicken curry dishes. The dish is very low in carbohydrate (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g), dominated by protein and fat from chicken and coconut. Coconut fat and dietary fibre from spices and coconut flesh attenuate glycemic response. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 9.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.9. Analogous GI reference: mixed meat curry with coconut milk, GI approximately 25–35 per University of Sydney GI Database category data.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the cumulative sodium from restaurant-style Kori Kundapuri can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated 30) result in a glycemic load of approximately 2.9 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kori Kundapuri is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, as its low carbohydrate content and high protein-fat ratio produce a minimal postprandial glycemic response; pairing with low-GI rice or rotti is preferable to white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 5.8 g per 100 g, primarily from coconut oil and coconut flesh, contributes to saturated fat intake; a 300 g serving provides approximately 17.4 g saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Kori Kundapuri due to its high saturated fat content from coconut, limiting intake to occasional servings and balancing with unsaturated fat sources elsewhere in the diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kori Kundapuri is a traditional boneless chicken curry originating from Kundapura (also spelled Kundapur), a coastal town in the Udupi district of Karnataka, South India. The dish is a hallmark of Tulu Nadu cuisine and is closely associated with the Mangalorean culinary tradition. 'Kori' means chicken in the Tulu language, and 'Kundapuri' denotes the Kundapura style of preparation. The curry is distinguished by its rich, deep-red coconut-based gravy, which is prepared by dry-roasting whole spices including coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, cloves, and cinnamon, then grinding them with freshly grated coconut and Byadagi red chillies — a variety prized for its vivid colour and moderate heat. Tamarind provides a characteristic sourness, while fresh coriander leaves and curry leaves add aromatic freshness. Boneless chicken pieces are simmered in this masala until tender, absorbing the complex spice profile. The dish is traditionally cooked in coconut oil, which contributes to its distinctive coastal flavour. Nutritionally, Kori Kundapuri is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The saturated fat content is elevated due to coconut, which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular risk. Sodium levels are moderate to high in restaurant preparations. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is classically served with Kori Rotti (thin, crispy rice wafers) or neer dosa, and is a popular main course in South Indian coastal restaurants across Singapore and the Indian diaspora.
common_user_queries: What does Kori mean in Tulu?
Is Kori Kundapuri suitable for diabetics?

What is Kundapuri chicken curry served with?
What spices are in Kundapuri chicken curry?

ingredient_synonyms.kundapuri: of or from Kundapura, coastal Karnataka
ingredient_synonyms.byadagi chilli: Byadagi red chilli, Kaddi chilli, mild red chilli variety from Karnataka

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Kori Kundapuri exists. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved ICMR-NIN entry for coconut-based chicken curry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category data for low-carbohydrate coconut chicken curries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source precisely matches this specific regional preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), coconut-based chicken curry entry, as no Kundapuri-specific entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous coconut-based chicken curry data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kori Kundapuri is available in the approved GI literature. GL per 300 g serving calculated as (30 × 9.6) / 100 = 2.9. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in traditional coconut oil and chicken preparations per ICMR-NIN data. 7. The dish is served at Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine (South Bridge Road, Singapore) as a chef recommendation item at SGD 18.90. Despite the restaurant's 'North Indian' branding, Kori Kundapuri is a South Indian coastal Karnataka dish. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Indian restaurant in Singapore); formal halal certification not verified from the input data. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore.
